metadata.summary: Codify and enforce Kubernetes admission policies using Kyverno or OPA/Gatekeeper. Start in audit mode, write unit tests with kyverno test / conftest, then enable enforcement.

content.title: Policy as Code: OPA, Kyverno, and Admission Control
content.summary: Policy as Code codifies compliance and security requirements in version-controlled, testable artifacts enforced by admission controllers. 96% of technical decision-makers say Policy as Code is vital for secure, scalable cloud operations. Use Kyverno (YAML, Kubernetes-native, easier) or OPA/Gatekeeper (Rego, multi-system, steeper curve). Always start in audit mode; write unit tests; enforce only after fixing existing violations.

why.p: Without admission control, any workload can be deployed with privileged containers, root users, unlimited resources, or images from untrusted registries — regardless of what SAST and container scanning found. Policy as Code closes this gap by evaluating every kubectl apply / helm install against a set of rules before the resource enters etcd. Policies live in Git, go through PR review, and get version-controlled just like application code.

Policy-as-code drift is the primary failure mode: policies authored at the platform team level become out of sync with what app teams actually deploy. Prevent this with a contract-test approach: app team PRs include fixture manifests that run through the policy engine in CI, ensuring policies and apps evolve together.

list.item: Kubernetes clusters running multi-team workloads where each team should not be able to override security baselines.
Compliance mandates that require no privileged containers, no root users, trusted registries only, required resource limits.
Multi-system policy enforcement beyond Kubernetes (OPA with Terraform, API gateway, CI/CD gates via Conftest).
Image signing verification — Kyverno verifyImages validates Sigstore cosign signatures at admission time.
Non-Kubernetes workloads — use native IAM policies, network ACLs, or Terraform Sentinel instead.
Replacing application-level security — admission control governs the platform, not the app's own auth/authz.
Enforcing policies before audit mode has been run and violations remediated — enforcement without a clean baseline blocks legitimate workloads.

rule.statement: Deploy policy engine in audit mode first. Review all audit violations and remediate existing resources before switching validationFailureAction to Enforce.
rule.rationale: Enabling enforcement before fixing existing violations blocks all pending deployments in all teams simultaneously, causing incidents and eroding trust in the security programme.

rule.statement: Every policy MUST have unit tests: `kyverno test` for Kyverno policies, `conftest verify` for OPA/Rego. Tests MUST run in CI alongside application tests.
rule.rationale: Policy author hallucinations are common — LLMs emit Kyverno YAML that uses non-existent fields or wrong API versions. Without automated tests, broken policies reach the cluster silently.

rule.statement: Version-control all policies in Git. Apply policies to the cluster via GitOps (ArgoCD, Flux) — never with kubectl apply from a local machine.
rule.rationale: Manual kubectl apply bypasses PR review, audit trail, and rollback capability. A GitOps sync loop ensures cluster state always matches the repository.

section.name: kyverno-core-policies
section.p: The baseline Kyverno policy set that every production cluster should enforce:

example.title: Kyverno baseline security ClusterPolicy
example.code:
# kyverno/policies/baseline-security.yaml apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1 kind: ClusterPolicy metadata: name: baseline-security annotations: policies.kyverno.io/title: Baseline Security Policy policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security policies.kyverno.io/severity: high policies.kyverno.io/description: >- Enforces baseline security requirements for all pods. spec: validationFailureAction: Enforce background: true rules: - name: deny-privileged match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Privileged containers are not allowed." pattern: spec: containers: - securityContext: privileged: "false" =(initContainers): - securityContext: privileged: "false"
- name: require-run-as-non-root match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Containers must run as non-root user." pattern: spec: securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true containers: - securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true
- name: deny-privilege-escalation match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Privilege escalation is not allowed." pattern: spec: containers: - securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false =(initContainers): - securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
- name: drop-all-capabilities match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Containers must drop all capabilities." pattern: spec: containers: - securityContext: capabilities: drop: - ALL =(initContainers): - securityContext: capabilities: drop: - ALL
- name: require-readonly-rootfs match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Root filesystem must be read-only." pattern: spec: containers: - securityContext: readOnlyRootFilesystem: true

example.title: Kyverno require resource limits
example.code: apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1 kind: ClusterPolicy metadata: name: require-resource-limits spec: validationFailureAction: Enforce background: true rules: - name: require-limits match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "CPU and memory limits are required." pattern: spec: containers: - resources: limits: memory: "?*" cpu: "?*"

example.title: Kyverno restrict image registries and disallow latest tag
example.code:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1 kind: ClusterPolicy metadata: name: restrict-image-registries spec: validationFailureAction: Enforce background: true rules: - name: validate-registries match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Images must be from trusted registries." pattern: spec: containers: - image: "gcr.io/* | docker.io/myorg/* | ghcr.io/myorg/*" =(initContainers): - image: "gcr.io/* | docker.io/myorg/* | ghcr.io/myorg/*"
--- apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1 kind: ClusterPolicy metadata: name: disallow-latest-tag spec: validationFailureAction: Enforce background: true rules: - name: disallow-latest match: any: - resources: kinds: - Pod validate: message: "Using 'latest' tag is not allowed. Specify a version tag." pattern: spec: containers: - image: "!*:latest" =(initContainers): - image: "!*:latest"

section.name: opa-rego-policies
section.p: OPA/Rego policies for multi-system enforcement (Kubernetes via Gatekeeper, Terraform via Conftest, CI gates):

example.title: OPA Rego — no privileged containers
OPA Rego — trusted registry enforcement
OPA Rego unit tests
OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate
example.code:
# gatekeeper/templates/k8srequiredlabels.yaml apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1 kind: ConstraintTemplate metadata: name: k8srequiredlabels spec: crd: spec: names: kind: K8sRequiredLabels validation: openAPIV3Schema: type: object properties: labels: type: array items: type: string targets: - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh rego: | package k8srequiredlabels
violation[{"msg": msg, "details": {"missing_labels": missing}}] { provided := {label | input.review.object.metadata.labels[label]} required := {label | label := input.parameters.labels[_]} missing := required - provided count(missing) > 0 msg := sprintf("Missing required labels: %v", [missing]) } --- # gatekeeper/constraints/require-labels.yaml apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1 kind: K8sRequiredLabels metadata: name: require-team-label spec: match: kinds: - apiGroups: [""] kinds: ["Namespace", "Pod"] excludedNamespaces: ["kube-system", "gatekeeper-system"] parameters: labels: - "team" - "environment"

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Write an OPA Rego policy that enforces [requirement].
Context: - Target: [Kubernetes/Terraform/CI/API] - Input format: [describe input JSON structure] - Requirement: [detailed policy requirement]
Include: 1. Package declaration 2. Default deny rule 3. Allow/deny rules with clear logic 4. Helper functions if needed 5. Unit tests 6. Documentation comments

Convert these security requirements to Kyverno policies:
Requirements: 1. [requirement 1] 2. [requirement 2] 3. [requirement 3]
For each policy include: - Policy metadata and annotations - Match/exclude criteria - Validation or mutation rules - Meaningful error messages - Background scan configuration

Debug this Rego policy that isn't working as expected:
```rego [paste policy] ```
Input that should [pass/fail]: ```json [paste input] ```
Expected behavior: [describe] Actual behavior: [describe]
Provide: 1. Issue identification 2. Fixed policy 3. Explanation of the fix 4. Test cases to verify

checklist.check: Choose policy engine: OPA/Gatekeeper (complex multi-system policies) or Kyverno (Kubernetes-native, YAML, easier).
Deploy policy engine to cluster in audit mode first (validationFailureAction: Audit).
Review all audit violations and fix existing non-compliant resources before enforcement.
Write unit tests: `kyverno test` for Kyverno, `conftest verify` for OPA/Rego.
Add policy CI tests: fixture manifests that pass/fail the policy engine, run in PR pipeline.
Document all policies with examples of compliant and non-compliant resources.
Version-control all policies; apply via GitOps (ArgoCD/Flux) — never manual kubectl apply.
Review policy violations weekly; update policies based on new threats.
Maintain an exception register with expiry dates; no permanent waivers.
Enable enforcement gradually per namespace — start with non-production namespaces.

section.name: core-policies-checklist
section.p: Minimum policy set for every production Kubernetes cluster:

list.item: Drop all capabilities; add only what is needed.
Trusted registries only (no Docker Hub public images).
No secrets in environment variables (use mounted secrets or External Secrets Operator).

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Policy author hallucinations: LLMs emit Kyverno that uses fields that don't exist or wrong API versions; always require `kyverno test` in CI before merging any policy PR.

Policy-as-code drift: policies authored at the platform team level become out of sync with what app teams actually deploy. Use a contract-test approach where app team PRs include fixture manifests validated against the policy engine.
